subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
subtopic_heading: Automatic Registration of Support Orders with FRO
fact_pattern: A court makes a child support order requiring David to pay $1,200/month. Neither party asks about the Family Responsibility Office at the hearing. David asks his lawyer whether he must pay support through the FRO or whether he can pay his former spouse directly without involving the FRO.
call_of_question: Under the Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996 (FRSAEA), are support orders automatically filed with the Family Responsibility Office?

options.A: Support orders are filed with the FRO only if the recipient requests enrollment; otherwise direct payment between the parties is the default.
options.B: Under FRSAEA s.3, every support order made by an Ontario court is automatically filed with the Director of the Family Responsibility Office; David must make payments through the FRO unless the parties have filed a joint notice to withdraw from the program.
options.C: Support orders are filed with the FRO only if the payor is in arrears at the time the order is made.
options.D: The FRO is optional; there is no automatic filing of any support order without the parties' express consent.

explanation: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, s.3(1) provides that every support order made by a court in Ontario shall be filed with the Director of the Family Responsibility Office immediately upon being made. This filing is automatic and mandatory — it does not require either party's request or consent. Once filed, the Director takes over enforcement of the order, and payments must be made to the FRO (not directly to the recipient) unless the parties have filed a joint withdrawal under s.7.
why_A_wrong: Registration with the FRO is not dependent on the recipient's request. FRSAEA s.3(1) mandates automatic filing of every Ontario support order. The FRO does not operate on an opt-in basis.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FRSAEA s.3(1) requires automatic filing of all Ontario support orders with the FRO Director; payments must be made through FRO unless the parties opt out under s.7.
why_C_wrong: Automatic filing applies to all support orders at the time they are made, not only to orders where the payor is already in arrears. FRSAEA s.3(1) contains no such qualification.
why_D_wrong: The FRO is not optional; it is the mandatory default enforcement regime for all Ontario support orders under FRSAEA s.3. Parties may opt out by following the withdrawal procedure under s.7, but FRO enrollment is automatic otherwise.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Automatic Registration — FRSAEA s.3
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 3(1)

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
subtopic_heading: Enforcement Mechanisms — Garnishment and Driver's Licence Suspension
fact_pattern: Nathan has accumulated $18,000 in child support arrears. The FRO has been unable to collect payment. Nathan is employed and earns $5,000/month net salary. He also has a valid Ontario driver's licence. The FRO is considering what enforcement tools to use.
call_of_question: Which of the following FRO enforcement mechanisms are available against Nathan in these circumstances?

options.A: The FRO may only apply to court for a contempt order; other enforcement tools require a court application.
options.B: The FRO may garnish Nathan's wages under FRSAEA s.14 and may suspend Nathan's driver's licence under FRSAEA s.34 without a court order; both mechanisms are available to the Director where there are arrears.
options.C: The FRO may garnish Nathan's wages but must obtain a court order specifically authorizing each and every garnishment.
options.D: The FRO cannot enforce child support arrears; the recipient must personally pursue enforcement through the courts.

explanation: The FRSAEA gives the Director of the FRO extensive enforcement powers to collect support arrears without requiring individual court orders for each enforcement action. Under FRSAEA s.14, the Director may serve a notice of garnishment on Nathan's employer to divert a portion of his wages to satisfy arrears. Under FRSAEA s.34, where a payor is in default of a support order, the Director may serve notice on the Registrar of Motor Vehicles to suspend the payor's driver's licence. Both tools are available to the Director based on the existing arrears, without the need for further court proceedings.
why_A_wrong: The FRO has extensive self-executing enforcement powers under the FRSAEA that do not require court orders. Garnishment (s.14) and driver's licence suspension (s.34) are administrative tools the Director can use directly.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FRSAEA s.14 authorizes wage garnishment and s.34 authorizes driver's licence suspension by the Director without a court order where there are support arrears.
why_C_wrong: Under FRSAEA s.14, the Director may issue garnishment notices directly without a separate court order for each garnishment. The Director's general authority to enforce is based on the existing support order, not case-by-case court authorization.
why_D_wrong: The FRO Director is specifically mandated to enforce support orders on behalf of recipients. Enforcement is the FRO's core function; recipients do not need to pursue enforcement personally once a support order is filed with the FRO.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: FRO Enforcement — Garnishment s.14 and Driver's Licence Suspension s.34
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 14 (garnishment), s. 34 (driver's licence suspension)

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
fact_pattern: Carlos has been in default of his support order for over a year and owes $25,000 in arrears. He has ignored FRO's garnishment efforts. He is self-employed and does not have a traditional employer. The Director brings a motion for a default hearing. Carlos asks what can happen to him at a default hearing.
call_of_question: What orders may a court make at a default hearing under FRSAEA s.37?

options.A: A default hearing is a mediation session; the court cannot make any enforcement order at the hearing.
options.B: At a default hearing, the court may only order the payor to pay a lump sum equal to all arrears immediately; no other remedy is available.
options.C: Under FRSAEA s.37, at a default hearing the court may make a range of orders including ordering the payor to pay arrears by installments, imposing a recognizance, requiring security for payment, incarcerating the payor for up to 180 days, or making any other appropriate enforcement order.
options.D: A default hearing is available only where the payor has been served by personal service; substituted service is not permitted.

explanation: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.37(11) provides that at a default hearing, the court has a wide range of remedies including: ordering payment of arrears by specified installments; requiring the payor to post security; requiring the payor to enter into a recognizance; and — as a last resort — incarcerating the payor for up to 180 days. Incarceration is not automatic; it is a remedy of last resort where the court finds the payor is able to pay but willfully refuses to do so (civil contempt in the support context). The default hearing is a court proceeding with real enforcement consequences, not a mediation.
why_A_wrong: A default hearing is a formal court proceeding under FRSAEA s.37, not a mediation. The court has broad authority to make enforceable orders, up to and including incarceration.
why_B_wrong: The court's jurisdiction at a default hearing is not limited to ordering a lump sum payment. Section 37(11) provides numerous alternative remedies, including installments, security, recognizance, and incarceration, which may be more appropriate where a lump sum is impossible.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FRSAEA s.37(11) gives the court a broad range of enforcement orders at a default hearing, including installment orders, security, recognizance, and up to 180 days incarceration as a last resort.
why_D_wrong: The FRSAEA does not limit service at default hearings to personal service only. The Rules of Civil Procedure applicable to family proceedings permit substituted service in appropriate circumstances.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Default Hearing — FRSAEA s.37
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 37(11)

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
subtopic_heading: Opting Out of FRO — Joint Notice Requirement Under FRSAEA s.7
fact_pattern: After their divorce, Susan and Paul have an amicable relationship and prefer to handle child support payments directly between themselves without FRO involvement. They both want to opt out of the FRO. Paul assumes that because he is making payments voluntarily, there is no need to formally notify the FRO.
call_of_question: Under the FRSAEA, what is required for the parties to opt out of FRO enforcement?

options.A: Either party may unilaterally opt out by filing a notice with the FRO; no consent from the other party is required.
options.B: Voluntary payment automatically removes the obligation to pay through FRO; no formal opt-out procedure is required.
options.C: Under FRSAEA s.7, a support order may be withdrawn from the FRO only upon the filing of a joint notice signed by both the payor and the recipient, indicating that the parties are aware of the consequences of withdrawal; the notice must be filed with the Director.
options.D: Opting out of FRO requires a court order; the parties cannot withdraw from the program by agreement alone.

explanation: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.7(1) provides that a support order may be withdrawn from the Director's enforcement upon the filing of a joint notice with the Director, signed by both the payor and the recipient, indicating that the parties are aware of the consequences of withdrawing from the Director's enforcement. Both parties must consent and sign; neither can unilaterally withdraw from the FRO. Simply making voluntary payments does not exempt the parties from the FRO scheme — a formal joint withdrawal notice is required.
why_A_wrong: A unilateral opt-out by one party is not permitted under FRSAEA s.7(1). Both the payor and the recipient must sign the joint notice. One party alone cannot withdraw the order from FRO enforcement.
why_B_wrong: Voluntary payment does not constitute a valid opt-out from the FRO. The support order remains registered with and enforceable by the FRO until a valid joint withdrawal under s.7 is filed. Informal direct payment arrangements do not discharge the registration.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FRSAEA s.7(1) requires a joint notice signed by both parties filed with the Director; only then is the support order withdrawn from FRO enforcement.
why_D_wrong: Opting out of FRO does not require a court order. The FRSAEA provides a statutory procedure — the joint notice under s.7 — that allows the parties to withdraw by agreement without court involvement.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Opting Out of FRO — FRSAEA s.7
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 7(1)

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
subtopic_heading: Limitation Period and Interest on Support Arrears
fact_pattern: Linda has not received child support payments from her former husband for 8 years. She wants to bring an enforcement action for all accumulated arrears. Her husband argues that the two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars most of the claims, and that interest does not run on support arrears.
call_of_question: What is the applicable limitation period for enforcement of support arrears in Ontario, and does interest run on arrears?

options.A: The two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to each missed support payment; therefore, Linda may only recover arrears from the past two years.
options.B: There is no limitation period for the enforcement of support arrears in Ontario; support arrears may be enforced regardless of how old they are, and interest accrues on arrears from the date each payment was due.
options.C: Support arrears are extinguished after five years under the Limitations Act, 2002; Linda can only collect arrears from the past five years.
options.D: Support arrears may only be collected for the period during which the child was under age 18; arrears relating to payments owed while the child was an adult are unenforceable.

explanation: Under the Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, support arrears are not subject to the general limitation periods in the Limitations Act, 2002. Ontario courts have held that claims to enforce support orders (including recovery of arrears) are exempt from the general two-year limitation period because they are ongoing court orders, not new claims. The Limitations Act, 2002 s.16(1)(c) provides that there is no limitation period for an order of the court. Interest accrues on each missed support payment from the date it was due, as each payment becomes a judgment debt once missed. Linda may seek enforcement of all accumulated arrears, subject to the court's discretion to address any laches or delay issues.
why_A_wrong: The two-year limitation period in the Limitations Act, 2002 does not apply to support arrears enforcement. Support orders are court orders and fall within the s.16(1)(c) exception — no limitation period applies to enforcement of court orders.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. There is no limitation period for enforcing support arrears in Ontario (Limitations Act, 2002 s.16(1)(c) exception for court orders); interest runs from the date each payment was due.
why_C_wrong: A five-year limitation period does not apply to support arrears in Ontario. The Limitations Act, 2002 s.16(1)(c) exempts enforcement of court orders, including support orders, from all limitation periods.
why_D_wrong: The enforceability of support arrears is not limited to amounts that accrued while the child was under 18. Arrears for adult child support (where a valid order existed) are equally enforceable. The limitation relates to the type of claim, not the age of the child when the payment was due.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Arrears — Limitation Period and Interest
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16(1)(c)

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
subtopic_heading: Driver's Licence Suspension — FRSAEA s.34 Procedure and Reinstatement
fact_pattern: Terrence has accumulated $9,000 in child support arrears over 18 months. He is currently employed as a courier and holds a valid Ontario driver's licence. The FRO Director has served notice under FRSAEA s.34 that his driver's licence will be suspended unless he pays the arrears or makes arrangements. Terrence argues that suspending a courier driver's licence is disproportionate and requests a hardship exemption.
call_of_question: Under FRSAEA s.34, what is the process for driver's licence suspension for support arrears, and how may a payor challenge the suspension?

options.A: The FRO must obtain a court order before suspending a driver's licence; the Director cannot act unilaterally.
options.B: Under FRSAEA s.34, the Director may give a payor notice of intention to have the Registrar suspend the payor's driver's licence; the payor then has 30 days to pay the arrears in full or make an arrangement satisfactory to the Director; if neither occurs, the Director may direct the Registrar to suspend the licence; the payor may apply to court for an order respecting the notice.
options.C: Driver's licence suspension is automatic the moment arrears accumulate; no notice is required.
options.D: Driver's licence suspension applies only where arrears exceed $20,000; Terrence's arrears of $9,000 are below the threshold.

explanation: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.34 provides a two-step process: (1) the Director gives the payor written notice of the intention to have the driver's licence suspended; (2) the payor has 30 days from the notice to pay the arrears in full or make arrangements satisfactory to the Director; if the payor fails to do so, the Director may direct the Registrar of Motor Vehicles to suspend the licence. The payor may apply to court to challenge the notice or seek relief, but must do so within the notice period. There is no minimum arrears threshold in the statute for licence suspension — the Director has broad discretion. Terrence's hardship argument (employment as a courier) would be presented to the court on any such application.
why_A_wrong: The FRO Director does not need a court order to initiate the s.34 licence suspension process. The process is administrative: the Director gives notice and, if the payor does not comply, directs the Registrar to suspend the licence.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FRSAEA s.34 provides a 30-day notice process followed by licence suspension if the payor fails to pay or arrange; the payor may apply to court to challenge the notice.
why_C_wrong: Driver's licence suspension is not automatic upon accumulation of arrears. FRSAEA s.34 requires a notice process giving the payor an opportunity to pay or arrange before suspension occurs.
why_D_wrong: No minimum arrears threshold of $20,000 or any other amount is prescribed in FRSAEA s.34 for driver's licence suspension. The Director has discretion to use this mechanism for any amount of arrears.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Driver's Licence Suspension — FRSAEA s.34
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 34

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
topic_heading: Contempt of Court for Breach of Support Order
subtopic_heading: Contempt of Court — Breach of Support Order Outside FRSAEA
fact_pattern: A court order requires Samuel to pay $2,500/month in spousal support. Samuel has paid nothing for 8 months despite having the financial means to do so. His former spouse seeks to bring contempt of court proceedings against him, in addition to FRO enforcement. Samuel argues that contempt proceedings are unavailable because the FRO handles all support enforcement.
call_of_question: Is contempt of court available as an enforcement mechanism for breach of a support order, in addition to FRO enforcement?

options.A: Samuel is correct — the FRSAEA creates a complete code for support enforcement; contempt of court is unavailable once FRO enrollment exists.
options.B: Contempt of court is available as a remedy for wilful breach of a support order, but requires a court application demonstrating the payor's intentional and contumacious refusal to comply despite having the means to do so; it operates concurrently with and does not exclude FRO enforcement.
options.C: Contempt of court applies only to parenting order violations, not support order violations.
options.D: Contempt of court for support violations carries only a monetary fine; incarceration is not available.

explanation: Contempt of court is an inherent power of the Superior Court of Justice, available for wilful disobedience of any court order, including a spousal support order. It is not displaced or limited by the FRSAEA enforcement regime. Contempt proceedings require the moving party to establish beyond a reasonable doubt that the payor had knowledge of the order, had the ability to comply, and wilfully chose not to comply (contumacious disobedience). Penalties for civil contempt include fines and, ultimately, imprisonment. FRO enforcement and contempt proceedings are complementary remedies that may be pursued concurrently — the FRO handles administrative enforcement while contempt addresses wilful non-compliance as a more serious judicial sanction.
why_A_wrong: The FRSAEA does not displace the court's inherent contempt jurisdiction. Both FRO enforcement and contempt proceedings are available, and they are complementary remedies. The FRSAEA regime does not create a code that excludes contempt.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Contempt of court is available for wilful breach of a support order, concurrently with FRO enforcement; it requires proof of intentional non-compliance despite means to pay.
why_C_wrong: Contempt of court is available for breach of any court order, including support orders. It is not limited to parenting order violations. A support order is a court order; wilful disobedience may be treated as contempt.
why_D_wrong: Contempt of court for civil contempt (wilful disobedience of a court order) may include imprisonment as a sanction. Courts impose incarceration where lesser remedies are insufficient to secure compliance, particularly for contumacious and continuing non-compliance.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Contempt of Court — Support Order Enforcement
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 31

tested_concepts: contempt available for support order breach

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
subtopic_heading: Inter-Provincial Support Enforcement — RESA and Reciprocal Enforcement
fact_pattern: An Ontario court made a child support order two years ago. The payor has since relocated to British Columbia. The recipient, who remains in Ontario, has unpaid support arrears of $15,000 and cannot afford to litigate in British Columbia. She asks the Ontario FRO whether the Ontario support order can be enforced against the payor who is now living in BC.
call_of_question: How may an Ontario support order be enforced against a payor who has relocated to another province?

options.A: An Ontario support order cannot be enforced against a payor living in another province; the recipient must obtain a new order from a BC court.
options.B: Under the Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002 (ISO Act) and the Reciprocal Enforcement of Support Orders (RESA) framework, Ontario support orders may be registered and enforced in other Canadian provinces; the FRO may coordinate with the BC equivalent office to enforce the Ontario order against the payor without the recipient needing to litigate in BC.
options.C: The Ontario FRO can enforce only in Ontario; the recipient must personally attend BC court and obtain a BC enforcement order.
options.D: Inter-provincial enforcement is available only for child support orders, not spousal support orders.

explanation: Ontario's Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002 (ISO Act) and the reciprocal enforcement framework allow Ontario support orders to be recognized and enforced in other provinces. The Ontario FRO may transmit the support order to the BC Family Maintenance Enforcement Program (FMEP) for enforcement in that province. The recipient does not need to retain BC counsel or attend BC court. Similarly, support obligations may be varied by courts in either province through the ISO Act process. This scheme prevents payors from escaping their obligations by relocating to another province.
why_A_wrong: Inter-provincial enforcement mechanisms specifically exist to address this situation. The ISO Act and RESA framework allow Ontario orders to be enforced in BC and vice versa; the recipient is not required to obtain a new BC order.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The ISO Act and RESA framework allow the Ontario FRO to coordinate with BC enforcement authorities to enforce the Ontario order against the payor without BC litigation by the recipient.
why_C_wrong: The FRO can coordinate inter-provincial enforcement through the ISO Act and RESA framework. The recipient does not need to personally attend BC court or obtain a separate BC enforcement order.
why_D_wrong: The ISO Act and RESA framework apply to all support orders — both child support and spousal support — made under Ontario legislation or the Divorce Act. There is no restriction to child support only.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Inter-Provincial Enforcement — ISO Act and RESA
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 13; Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31

tested_concepts: no need for new BC order

subject: family_law
chapter_title: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
fact_pattern: Darius owes $12,000 in child support arrears. The FRO has already suspended his driver's licence, but he continues to drive using a vehicle registered in his corporation's name. The FRO is considering additional enforcement tools. A colleague tells Darius that because the vehicle is in the corporation's name, the FRO cannot suspend the vehicle registration.
call_of_question: Does the FRSAEA provide for suspension of vehicle registrations (licence plates), and does a corporate registration insulate the vehicle?

options.A: The FRSAEA authorizes suspension only of the payor's personal driver's licence, not vehicle registrations; corporate registrations are fully exempt.
options.B: Under FRSAEA s.35, the Director may also direct the Registrar to refuse to validate or to suspend a vehicle permit (licence plate) held by the payor; however, a vehicle registered in the name of a corporation that the payor controls may be scrutinized, and courts have looked past corporate registration in egregious cases.
options.C: Vehicle registration suspension is automatic once a driver's licence is suspended; no additional FRO step is required.
options.D: The FRO has no authority over vehicle registrations in any circumstances.

explanation: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.35 provides that the Director may direct the Registrar of Motor Vehicles to refuse to validate or to suspend a vehicle permit (commonly known as a licence plate) held by the payor. This is a separate enforcement mechanism from driver's licence suspension under s.34. The suspension of vehicle permits may target vehicles registered in the payor's personal name. Where a vehicle is registered in a corporation's name, the effectiveness of this mechanism depends on whether the court or Director can pierce the corporate veil — an issue that arises in egregious cases of enforcement avoidance. Simply registering a vehicle in a corporation the payor controls does not automatically insulate it from enforcement scrutiny.
why_A_wrong: FRSAEA s.35 expressly provides for suspension of vehicle permits (licence plates), not only driver's licences. Both s.34 (driver's licence) and s.35 (vehicle permits) are available enforcement mechanisms.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FRSAEA s.35 authorizes vehicle permit suspension against the payor; corporate registration may be scrutinized in egregious avoidance cases.
why_C_wrong: Vehicle registration suspension under s.35 is a separate step from driver's licence suspension under s.34. The FRO must take distinct action under each provision; suspension of one does not automatically trigger the other.
why_D_wrong: FRSAEA s.35 expressly grants the FRO Director authority over vehicle permits (licence plates). The Director has broad enforcement powers including both driver's licence and vehicle registration suspension.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.heading: Vehicle Permit Suspension — FRSAEA s.35
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 35
